items.title: Why Whipple's triad is required to diagnose hypoglycemia
items.content:
Hypoglycemic symptoms (sweating, tremor, confusion) are nonspecific and common, so a low glucose value alone — or symptoms alone — is not enough. Whipple's triad requires ALL THREE: (1) symptoms consistent with hypoglycemia, (2) a low plasma glucose measured by a precise method, and (3) resolution of symptoms when glucose is raised.

This matters because a normal glucose during symptoms excludes hypoglycemia as the cause, and a low glucose without symptoms (or without relief on correction) may be artifact (pseudohypoglycemia). The fasting lower limit is ~70 mg/dL, but lower values occur normally late after meals, in pregnancy, and with prolonged (>24 h) fasting.
keyPoints: Need symptoms + low glucose + relief on raising glucose.
Normal glucose during symptoms rules hypoglycemia out.
Glucometers/CGM are not adequate for definitive diagnosis.
items.reference: Intro: "Hypoglycemia may be documented by Whipple's triad: (1) symptoms consistent with hypoglycemia, (2) a low plasma glucose concentration measured with a precise method, and (3) relief of symptoms after the plasma glucose level is raised."

items.title: How the body defends against falling glucose — in order
items.content:
The brain is an obligate glucose user and cannot store/synthesize glucose, so glucose must be defended in a stepwise sequence as it falls:
1. FIRST defense — a DECREASE in insulin secretion (allows glycogenolysis/gluconeogenesis to resume).
2. SECOND — glucagon rises (stimulates hepatic glycogenolysis/gluconeogenesis).
3. THIRD — epinephrine rises (stimulates hepatic/renal glucose output, limits peripheral uptake, drives lipolysis) — and becomes critical when glucagon is deficient.
4. Behavioral — symptoms prompt food intake.
Cortisol and growth hormone only support glucose after ~4 h and play NO role in acute hypoglycemia defense.
keyPoints: Order: ↓insulin → ↑glucagon → ↑epinephrine → symptoms/eating.
Epinephrine is critical when glucagon is deficient.
Cortisol/GH are not acute defenses.
items.reference: Counterregulation: "a decrease in insulin secretion is the first defense against hypoglycemia... pancreatic α-cell glucagon and adrenomedullary epinephrine play a primary role... cortisol and growth hormone play no role in defense against acute hypoglycemia."

items.title: Why hypoglycemia produces two distinct symptom types
items.content:
Symptoms fall into two mechanistic groups:
• NEUROGLYCOPENIC — direct CNS glucose deprivation: behavioral change, confusion, fatigue, seizure, loss of consciousness, arrhythmia, and (if severe) death.
• NEUROGENIC (autonomic) — perception of the sympathoadrenal discharge triggered by hypoglycemia: ADRENERGIC symptoms (palpitations, tremor, anxiety — largely norepinephrine) and CHOLINERGIC symptoms (sweating, hunger, paresthesias — acetylcholine).

Clinically, the autonomic symptoms are the early 'warning' that lets a patient treat themselves before neuroglycopenia impairs them — which is why losing them (hypoglycemia unawareness) is dangerous. Common signs: diaphoresis, pallor, tachycardia.
keyPoints: Neuroglycopenic = brain starvation (confusion, seizure, coma).
Neurogenic = sympathoadrenal: adrenergic (tremor/palpitations) + cholinergic (sweating/hunger).
Autonomic symptoms are the protective early warning.
items.reference: Clinical Manifestations: "Neuroglycopenic manifestations... are the direct result of central nervous system glucose deprivation... Neurogenic (or autonomic) manifestations... include adrenergic symptoms... such as palpitations, tremor, and anxiety, as well as cholinergic symptoms... such as sweating, hunger, and paresthesias."

items.title: Why glycemic thresholds for symptoms shift
items.content:
The glucose level at which counterregulation/symptoms begin is DYNAMIC, not fixed:
• In POORLY controlled diabetes, thresholds shift UPWARD — patients feel hypoglycemic symptoms as glucose merely falls toward normal.
• With RECURRENT hypoglycemia (intensively treated diabetes or insulinoma), thresholds shift DOWNWARD — symptoms appear only at very low glucose, so warning is delayed.

This adaptive shifting underlies hypoglycemia unawareness: repeated lows blunt the symptomatic response at higher glucose, perpetuating the problem.
keyPoints: Poor control → symptoms at near-normal glucose.
Recurrent lows → symptoms only at very low glucose (delayed warning).
Threshold shifting drives hypoglycemia unawareness.
items.reference: Counterregulation: "They shift to higher-than-normal glucose levels in people with poorly controlled diabetes... thresholds shift to lower-than-normal glucose levels in people with recurrent hypoglycemia."

items.title: How hypoglycemia-associated autonomic failure creates a vicious cycle
items.content:
Iatrogenic hypoglycemia in diabetes is rarely just 'too much insulin' — it reflects insulin excess PLUS failed defenses (HAAF):
• Defective glucose counterregulation: insulin doesn't fall (absolute deficiency), glucagon doesn't rise (lost after a few years in T1DM — a signaling defect, since alpha cells are still present), and the epinephrine response is attenuated.
• Hypoglycemia unawareness: the blunted sympathoadrenal (mainly sympathetic neural) response removes the warning symptoms.

Crucially, recent antecedent hypoglycemia (or prior exercise/sleep) blunts the epinephrine and symptom responses to the NEXT low — so each episode predisposes to the next. Defective counterregulation raises severe-hypoglycemia risk ≥25-fold; unawareness raises it 6-fold. Both are largely REVERSIBLE with 2–3 weeks of scrupulous hypoglycemia avoidance.
keyPoints: HAAF = defective counterregulation + hypoglycemia unawareness.
Antecedent hypoglycemia blunts the next response → vicious cycle.
Reversible by 2–3 weeks of avoiding hypoglycemia.
items.reference: HAAF: "recent antecedent iatrogenic hypoglycemia... causes both defective glucose counterregulation... and hypoglycemia unawareness... create a vicious cycle... reversible by as little as 2-3 weeks of scrupulous avoidance of hypoglycemia."

items.title: Why the glucagon defect in T1DM is functional, not structural
items.content:
In long-standing T1DM, glucagon fails to rise during hypoglycemia — yet the pancreatic alpha cells are present in normal number and size, and glucagon DOES respond to other stresses (e.g., exercise). This shows the defect is a SIGNALING (functional) problem specific to the hypoglycemia response, not destruction of alpha cells.

This distinguishes HAAF (functional, potentially reversible) from classic diabetic autonomic neuropathy (a structural, irreversible disorder that also impairs these responses). Recognizing the functional nature explains why hypoglycemia avoidance can restore awareness.
keyPoints: Alpha cells are intact; glucagon still responds to exercise.
The hypoglycemia-specific glucagon failure is a signaling defect.
HAAF (functional, reversible) ≠ autonomic neuropathy (structural, irreversible).
items.reference: Defective Counterregulation: "the defect that restricts glucagon release during hypoglycemia in T1DM... appears to be a signaling defect, as glucagon responses to other physiologic stress in T1DM (e.g., exercise) are preserved... distinct from classic diabetic autonomic neuropathy... a structural and irreversible disorder."

items.title: Why hypoglycemia is the limiting factor in diabetes management
items.content:
Hypoglycemia caps how tightly diabetes can be controlled. People with T1DM average ~2 symptomatic episodes per week and ≥1 severe (disabling) episode per year; an estimated 6–10% of people with T1DM die as a result of hypoglycemia.

Incidence is lower in T2DM, but prevalence in insulin-requiring T2DM approaches 70%, and because T2DM patients outnumber T1DM by 10–20 fold, the TOTAL burden of hypoglycemia is now greater in T2DM. Severe hypoglycemia can occur even at HbA1c >8%, and in T2DM it carries increased cardiovascular/cerebrovascular morbidity and mortality for up to a year — so glycemic improvement in high-risk patients must be incremental.
keyPoints: Hypoglycemia limits achievable glycemic control.
Total burden is now greater in T2DM (sheer numbers).
Severe hypoglycemia can occur at HbA1c >8% and raises CV risk for ~1 year.
items.reference: Hypoglycemia in Diabetes: "Hypoglycemia is the limiting factor in the glycemic management of diabetes mellitus... An estimated 6–10% of people with T1DM die as a result of hypoglycemia."

items.title: Which diabetes drugs cause hypoglycemia (and which don't)
items.content:
Only insulin and insulin SECRETAGOGUES (sulfonylureas, glinides) cause hypoglycemia on their own. Metformin, thiazolidinediones, α-glucosidase inhibitors, GLP-1 receptor agonists, SGLT-2 inhibitors, and DPP-4 inhibitors do NOT cause hypoglycemia by themselves.

However, these 'safe' agents DO increase hypoglycemia risk when COMBINED with a sulfonylurea, glinide, or insulin. As T2DM progresses to absolute insulin deficiency and requires complex insulin regimens, its hypoglycemia frequency approaches that of T1DM.
keyPoints: Hypoglycemia-causing: insulin, sulfonylureas, glinides.
Metformin/TZD/α-glucosidase/GLP-1RA/SGLT-2/DPP-4 don't cause it alone.
But they add risk when combined with insulin/secretagogue.
items.reference: Hypoglycemia in Diabetes: "Metformin, thiazolidinediones, α-glucosidase inhibitors, GLP-1 receptor agonists, sodium-glucose cotransporter 2 inhibitors, and DPP-IV inhibitors do not cause hypoglycemia. However, they increase the risk when combined with a sulfonylurea, glinide, or insulin."

items.title: Why alcohol causes hypoglycemia
items.content:
Ethanol blocks GLUCONEOGENESIS but NOT glycogenolysis. So alcohol-induced hypoglycemia typically occurs after a several-day binge during which the person eats little — glycogen stores become depleted, and the blocked gluconeogenesis can no longer maintain glucose.

Key clinical points: blood ethanol is usually measurable but correlates poorly with glucose; because gluconeogenesis becomes the dominant glucose source during prolonged hypoglycemia, alcohol can worsen hypoglycemia in insulin-treated diabetics. (Correspondingly, glucagon is ineffective in alcohol-induced hypoglycemia because glycogen is already depleted.)
keyPoints: Ethanol blocks gluconeogenesis, not glycogenolysis.
Hypoglycemia occurs after a binge with poor intake (glycogen depleted).
Glucagon won't work (no glycogen) — give glucose.
items.reference: Drugs: "Ethanol blocks gluconeogenesis but not glycogenolysis. Thus, alcohol-induced hypoglycemia typically occurs after a several-day ethanol binge during which the person eats little food, with consequent glycogen depletion."

items.title: How to diagnose endogenous hyperinsulinism biochemically
items.content:
The defining feature is failure of insulin secretion to fall during hypoglycemia. So you sample DURING hypoglycemia (glucose <55 mg/dL with symptoms) and look for INAPPROPRIATELY non-suppressed insulin and its co-products:
• insulin ≥3 µU/mL (≥18 pmol/L)
• C-peptide ≥0.6 ng/mL (≥0.2 nmol/L)
• proinsulin ≥5.0 pmol/L
Plus a LOW β-hydroxybutyrate (≤2.7 mmol/L) and a glucose rise >25 mg/dL after IV glucagon — both indicating insulin (or IGF) action is suppressing ketogenesis and preserving glycogen.

The values needn't be high by normal standards — they are abnormal because they should be suppressed when glucose is low.
keyPoints: Sample during hypoglycemia (glucose <55) with symptoms.
Inappropriately high insulin/C-peptide/proinsulin + low β-OHB + glucose rise after glucagon.
Also screen for oral hypoglycemic agents.
items.reference: Endogenous Hyperinsulinism: "Critical diagnostic findings are a plasma insulin concentration ≥3 μU/mL... a plasma C-peptide concentration ≥0.6 ng/mL... and a plasma proinsulin concentration ≥5.0 pmol/L when the plasma glucose concentration is <55 mg/dL."

items.title: Why C-peptide separates exogenous insulin from a secretagogue/insulinoma
items.content:
C-peptide is cleaved from proinsulin in equimolar amounts with endogenous insulin, but manufactured insulin contains NO C-peptide. So in hyperinsulinemic hypoglycemia:
• EXOGENOUS (injected) insulin → high insulin, LOW C-peptide (exogenous insulin suppresses the patient's own secretion).
• INSULINOMA or SULFONYLUREA/glinide → high insulin AND high C-peptide (the beta cell is secreting).

To distinguish an insulinoma from a secretagogue (both raise C-peptide), screen the blood/urine for oral hypoglycemic agents. Factitious hypoglycemia is most common in health-care workers, people with diabetes or their relatives, and those with other factitious illnesses.
keyPoints: Injected insulin: high insulin, LOW C-peptide.
Insulinoma/secretagogue: high insulin AND C-peptide.
Screen for sulfonylurea/glinide to separate drug from tumor.
items.reference: Factitious: "Ingestion of an insulin secretagogue causes hypoglycemia with increased C-peptide levels, whereas exogenous insulin causes hypoglycemia with low C-peptide levels, reflecting suppression of insulin secretion."

items.title: Insulinoma: features and localization
items.content:
Insulinoma is the prototype of endogenous hyperinsulinism: incidence ~1 in 250,000/year, >90% benign, and >99% within the pancreas, usually small (<2 cm in 90%). It presents because of hypoglycemia (not mass effect). Median age ~50 in sporadic cases, but ~3rd decade when part of MEN 1.

Localization: CT/MRI detect ~70–80%; endoscopic ultrasound is the most sensitive (~90%); selective arterial calcium stimulation regionalizes; intraoperative ultrasound almost always localizes. Treatment: surgical resection of a solitary tumor is curative; diazoxide (inhibits insulin secretion) or octreotide for unresectable disease (everolimus is an option).
keyPoints: >90% benign, >99% pancreatic, usually <2 cm.
MEN 1 association (younger, ~3rd decade).
Surgery curative; diazoxide/octreotide if unresectable; EUS most sensitive (~90%).
items.reference: Insulinoma: "Because >90% of insulinomas are benign, they are a treatable cause of potentially fatal hypoglycemia... Surgical resection of a solitary insulinoma is generally curative. Diazoxide... or the somatostatin analogue octreotide can be used."

items.title: Why large non-islet tumors cause hypoglycemia (big IGF-II)
items.content:
Large mesenchymal or epithelial tumors (e.g., hepatomas, adrenocortical carcinomas, carcinoids) can cause fasting hypoglycemia with a kinetic picture that LOOKS like hyperinsulinism — but insulin secretion is appropriately SUPPRESSED. The mechanism is overproduction of incompletely processed IGF-II ('big IGF-II'), which doesn't bind its carrier proteins normally and so reaches tissues to act like insulin.

Clues: the tumor is usually clinically apparent, the IGF-II:IGF-I ratio is high, and insulin/C-peptide are LOW (suppressed). Reducing tumor bulk (or glucocorticoid/GH) ameliorates hypoglycemia even when cure isn't possible.
keyPoints: Hypoglycemia with SUPPRESSED insulin/C-peptide → think 'big IGF-II'.
High IGF-II:IGF-I ratio; tumor usually obvious.
Debulking ± glucocorticoid/GH helps.
items.reference: Non-β-Cell Tumors: "hypoglycemia is due to overproduction of an incompletely processed form of insulin-like growth factor II ('big IGF-II')... plasma ratios of IGF-II to IGF-I are high."

items.title: Why glucagon fails in some hypoglycemia and octreotide is used in others
items.content:
Urgent treatment: if able, give 15–20 g oral glucose; if not (neuroglycopenia), give IV glucose (25 g) then an infusion. If no IV access, give glucagon 1 mg IM/SC.

But glucagon's mechanism is glycogenolysis, so it is INEFFECTIVE in glycogen-depleted patients (e.g., alcohol-induced hypoglycemia) and LESS useful in T2DM (it also stimulates insulin secretion). For SULFONYLUREA-induced hypoglycemia, octreotide suppresses insulin secretion and prevents the rebound. All these measures are transient — patients must eat to replete glycogen.
keyPoints: Oral glucose 15–20 g; IV glucose 25 g; glucagon 1 mg if no IV access.
Glucagon useless if glycogen-depleted (alcohol); less useful in T2DM.
Octreotide for sulfonylurea-induced hypoglycemia.
items.reference: Urgent Treatment: "Because it acts by stimulating glycogenolysis, glucagon is ineffective in glycogen-depleted individuals (e.g., those with alcohol-induced hypoglycemia)... The somatostatin analogue octreotide can be used to suppress insulin secretion in sulfonylurea-induced hypoglycemia."

items.title: Framing the differential: ill/medicated vs seemingly well
items.content:
The hypoglycemia differential is organized by clinical context:
• ILL or MEDICATED patient: think DRUGS first (insulin/secretagogue/alcohol — including surreptitious), then critical illness (hepatic/renal/cardiac failure, sepsis, inanition), hormone deficiency (cortisol/GH), and non-islet-cell tumor.
• SEEMINGLY WELL patient: think endogenous hyperinsulinism (insulinoma, nesidioblastosis, post-gastric-bypass, autoimmune) or accidental/surreptitious/malicious hypoglycemia.

Because hypoglycemia is rare without relevant drug-treated diabetes, a hypoglycemic disorder is diagnosed only when Whipple's triad is demonstrated.
keyPoints: Ill/medicated → drugs, critical illness, hormone deficiency, non-islet tumor.
Seemingly well → endogenous hyperinsulinism or factitious.
Drugs (esp. surreptitious) are always the first consideration.
items.reference: Hypoglycemia Without Diabetes: "the initial diagnostic focus should be on the possibility of drug involvement and then on critical illnesses, hormone deficiency, or non-islet cell tumor hypoglycemia... the focus should shift to possible endogenous hyperinsulinism."

items.title: Why critical illness causes hypoglycemia
items.content:
After drugs, serious illness is the commonest cause of hospital hypoglycemia:
• Liver failure (e.g., toxic hepatitis): the liver is the major site of glucose production, so extensive hepatic destruction causes fasting hypoglycemia.
• Renal failure: reduced insulin clearance (raising effective insulin) plus reduced mobilization of gluconeogenic precursors.
• Sepsis: cytokines increase glucose utilization in macrophage-rich tissues (liver, spleen, lung) and inhibit gluconeogenesis; combined with glycogen depletion and hypoperfusion, glucose production can't keep up.
• Cardiac failure: mechanism unclear (hepatic congestion/hypoxia).
• Inanition/starvation: glycogen depletion with only a modest counterregulatory response.
keyPoints: Liver failure → loss of glucose production.
Renal failure → reduced insulin clearance + fewer precursors.
Sepsis → cytokine-driven utilization + inhibited gluconeogenesis.
items.reference: Critical Illness: "serious illnesses such as renal, hepatic, or cardiac failure; sepsis; and inanition are second only to drugs as causes of hypoglycemia."

items.title: Why cortisol/GH deficiency rarely causes acute hypoglycemia
items.content:
Cortisol and growth hormone are NOT critical for preventing acute hypoglycemia in adults (they act late, >4 h, and contribute far less than epinephrine). So their deficiency causes hypoglycemia only with PROLONGED fasting or added stress — e.g., Addison's disease or hypopituitarism, where anorexia/weight loss deplete glycogen and impaired gluconeogenesis (cortisol deficiency) limits glucose production. GH deficiency can cause hypoglycemia in young children.

Isolated epinephrine deficiency (e.g., after bilateral adrenalectomy with adequate glucocorticoid) does NOT cause hypoglycemia, and neither does adrenergic blockade if other glucoregulatory systems are intact — except that combined glucagon AND epinephrine deficiency is central to iatrogenic hypoglycemia in insulin-deficient diabetes.
keyPoints: Cortisol/GH deficiency → hypoglycemia only with prolonged fast/stress.
Isolated epinephrine deficiency alone doesn't cause hypoglycemia.
Combined glucagon + epinephrine deficiency drives iatrogenic DM hypoglycemia.
items.reference: Hormone Deficiencies: "Neither cortisol nor growth hormone is critical to the prevention of hypoglycemia, at least in adults... hypoglycemia can occur with prolonged fasting in patients with primary adrenocortical failure (Addison's disease) or hypopituitarism."

items.title: Endogenous hyperinsulinism beyond insulinoma
items.content:
Not all endogenous hyperinsulinism is an insulinoma:
• Nesidioblastosis — diffuse islet involvement with beta-cell hypertrophy/hyperplasia, causing FASTING hypoglycemia.
• Noninsulinoma pancreatogenous hypoglycemia (NIPHS) — similar islet pattern but POSTPRANDIAL hypoglycemia.
• Post-gastric-bypass hypoglycemia — usually after Roux-en-Y, with diffuse islet involvement and postprandial hyperinsulinemic hypoglycemia (exaggerated GLP-1 response implicated).
• Insulin autoimmune hypoglycemia — antibody to insulin (binds then releases insulin, causing late postprandial lows) or an agonist antibody to the insulin receptor.
When these fail medical therapy (α-glucosidase inhibitor, diazoxide, octreotide), partial pancreatectomy may be needed.
keyPoints: Nesidioblastosis = fasting; NIPHS = postprandial.
Post-Roux-en-Y bypass → postprandial hyperinsulinemic hypoglycemia.
Autoimmune (anti-insulin or insulin-receptor antibodies) can mimic insulinoma.
items.reference: Endogenous Hyperinsulinism: "This pattern is commonly referred to as nesidioblastosis... a disorder termed noninsulinoma pancreaticogenous hypoglycemia. Post-gastric bypass postprandial hypoglycemia, which most often follows Roux-en-Y gastric bypass."

items.title: Why a low lab glucose can be an artifact
items.content:
A measured low glucose WITHOUT corresponding symptoms should raise suspicion of pseudohypoglycemia. Even with an accurate method, the blood's formed elements keep metabolizing glucose ex vivo after the sample is drawn — so glucose falls in the tube, especially with leukocytosis, erythrocytosis, or thrombocytosis, or when serum separation is delayed.

Also, hand-held glucometers and CGMs are not quantitative at low glucose and must NOT be used for the definitive diagnosis of hypoglycemia — draw a properly handled plasma sample.
keyPoints: Low glucose without symptoms → suspect artifact.
Ex-vivo glycolysis (leukocytosis/erythrocytosis/thrombocytosis, delayed processing) lowers the reading.
Glucometers/CGM aren't valid for definitive diagnosis.
items.reference: Factitious/Pseudohypoglycemia: "low measured glucose concentrations can be a virtual—e.g., the result of continued glucose metabolism by the formed elements of the blood ex vivo, particularly in the presence of leukocytosis, erythrocytosis, or thrombocytosis (pseudohypoglycemia)."

items.title: Why a period of hypoglycemia avoidance restores awareness
items.content:
Because HAAF is driven by recent antecedent hypoglycemia blunting the sympathoadrenal/symptom response, the counterintuitive treatment for hypoglycemia unawareness is to scrupulously AVOID hypoglycemia for 2–3 weeks. This resets the glycemic thresholds upward, restoring warning symptoms and partially the epinephrine response.

Practically, prevention also requires: reducing/retiming offending drugs, individualized (often relaxed) glycemic goals — 'the lowest HbA1c that does not cause severe hypoglycemia and preserves awareness' — flexible analogue insulin regimens, CGM (with low-glucose suspend), and structured patient education.
keyPoints: 2–3 weeks of avoiding lows reverses hypoglycemia unawareness.
Aim for the lowest HbA1c that avoids severe hypoglycemia and keeps awareness.
Use analogue insulins, CGM with predictive low-glucose suspend, and education.
items.reference: HAAF/Prevention: "Given a history of hypoglycemia unawareness, a 2- to 3-week period of scrupulous avoidance of hypoglycemia is indicated."

items.title: Why the brain depends on continuous glucose delivery
items.content:
Glucose is an obligate fuel for the brain under physiologic conditions: the brain cannot synthesize glucose and stores only a few minutes' worth as glycogen, so it needs a continuous arterial supply. When plasma glucose falls below the physiologic range, blood-to-brain glucose transport becomes inadequate to support brain energy metabolism — producing neuroglycopenic symptoms.

Between meals, glucose is maintained by hepatic glycogenolysis and hepatic/renal gluconeogenesis; hepatic glycogen lasts ~8 h (less with exercise, illness, or starvation). Gluconeogenesis needs low insulin plus counterregulatory hormones and substrate (lactate/amino acids from muscle, glycerol from fat).
keyPoints: Brain can't make/store much glucose → needs continuous supply.
Glycogen sustains glucose ~8 h; then gluconeogenesis dominates.
Prolonged hypoglycemia depends on gluconeogenesis (why alcohol is dangerous).
items.reference: Glucose Balance: "Glucose is an obligate metabolic fuel for the brain... The brain cannot synthesize glucose or store more than a few minutes' supply as glycogen and therefore requires a continuous supply of glucose."

items.title: Conventional risk factors for hypoglycemia in diabetes
items.content:
Conventional risk factors reflect relative or absolute insulin EXCESS: (1) insulin/secretagogue doses excessive, ill-timed, or wrong type; (2) reduced exogenous glucose influx (overnight/temporary fasting, missed meals); (3) increased insulin-independent glucose use (exercise); (4) increased insulin sensitivity (improved control, middle of the night, after exercise, weight loss/fitness); (5) reduced endogenous glucose production (alcohol); and (6) reduced insulin clearance (renal failure).

These alone explain only a minority of episodes — the compromised defenses of HAAF (absolute insulin deficiency, prior hypoglycemia/exercise/sleep, autonomic neuropathy, lower glycemic goals) account for most severe events.
keyPoints: Think insulin excess: dose/timing, fasting, exercise, ↑sensitivity, alcohol, renal failure.
Conventional factors explain only a minority of episodes.
HAAF factors drive most severe hypoglycemia.
items.reference: Conventional Risk Factors: "identified on the basis of relative or absolute insulin excess... insulin (or insulin secretagogue) doses are excessive, ill-timed, or of the wrong type... However, these conventional risk factors alone explain a minority of episodes."

items.title: Non-diabetes drugs that can cause hypoglycemia
items.content:
Beyond insulin/secretagogues and alcohol, several commonly used drugs are associated with hypoglycemia: ACE inhibitors and angiotensin-receptor blockers, β-adrenergic receptor antagonists, quinolone antibiotics, indomethacin, quinine, and sulfonamides.

Clinical relevance: in any patient with unexplained hypoglycemia — even without known diabetes drugs — drug involvement (including surreptitious, accidental, or malicious administration) should be the FIRST consideration. β-blockers also blunt adrenergic warning symptoms, masking hypoglycemia.
keyPoints: ACE-I/ARB, β-blockers, quinolones, quinine, indomethacin, sulfonamides.
Drugs are the first consideration in unexplained hypoglycemia.
β-blockers can mask the adrenergic warning symptoms.
items.reference: Drugs: "Many other drugs have been associated with hypoglycemia. These include... angiotensin-converting enzyme inhibitors and angiotensin receptor antagonists, β-adrenergic receptor antagonists, quinolone antibiotics, indomethacin, quinine, and sulfonamides."

items.title: Why severe hypoglycemia raises cardiovascular risk
items.content:
Hypoglycemia is not merely an inconvenience — it activates proinflammatory, procoagulant, and proatherothrombotic responses in T1DM, T2DM, and even non-diabetics: increased platelet aggregation, reduced fibrinolysis (raised PAI-1), increased intravascular coagulation, and reduced protective nitric-oxide-mediated vasodilation.

This explains why severe hypoglycemia carries increased cardiovascular/cerebrovascular morbidity and mortality for up to a year afterward, and why aggressively lowering HbA1c in high-CV-risk T2DM patients (risking hypoglycemia) can be harmful — improvements should be incremental.
keyPoints: Hypoglycemia is prothrombotic and impairs vasodilation.
Severe episodes raise CV/cerebrovascular risk for up to a year.
Lower HbA1c cautiously in high-CV-risk patients.
items.reference: Etiology and Pathophysiology: "Hypoglycemia activates proinflammatory, procoagulant, and proatherothrombotic responses... increase platelet aggregation, reduce fibrinolytic balance... and increase intravascular coagulation."

items.title: Inborn errors of metabolism causing hypoglycemia in adults
items.content:
Rarely, hypoglycemia in adults reflects inborn metabolic errors, classified by timing:
• FASTING hypoglycemia — glycogen storage diseases (types 0, I, III, IV; types I and III show high lactate, with high ketones in type III) and defects in fatty-acid oxidation or gluconeogenesis (fructose-1,6-bisphosphatase, causing lactic acidosis).
• POSTPRANDIAL — glucokinase/SUR1/Kir6.2 mutations, congenital glycosylation disorders, hereditary fructose intolerance.
• EXERCISE-INDUCED — hyperinsulinemia from increased monocarboxylate transporter 1 activity in beta cells.
keyPoints: Fasting: glycogen-storage disease, fatty-acid oxidation/gluconeogenesis defects.
Postprandial: K-channel mutations, hereditary fructose intolerance.
Exercise-induced: MCT1-driven hyperinsulinemia.
items.reference: Inborn Errors: "Fasting Hypoglycemia... disorders of glycogenolysis... Defects in fatty acid oxidation... Postprandial Hypoglycemia... glucokinase, SUR1, and Kir6.2 potassium channel mutations... Exercise-Induced Hypoglycemia... increased activity of monocarboxylate transporter 1."

items.title: Why you draw bloods before giving glucose
items.content:
When hypoglycemia is suspected, blood should be drawn (whenever possible) BEFORE giving glucose, so a low plasma glucose can be documented and — if the cause is obscure — insulin, C-peptide, proinsulin, and β-hydroxybutyrate can be measured and a sulfonylurea/glinide screen sent while the patient is still hypoglycemic.

This is the only reliable window to capture the diagnostic biochemistry; once glucose is given, the values are no longer interpretable. Treatment should not be delayed for a critically ill patient, but a baseline sample first preserves the diagnosis.
keyPoints: Draw the diagnostic sample before treating, when possible.
Key labs during the low: insulin, C-peptide, proinsulin, β-OHB, secretagogue screen.
After glucose is given, the biochemistry is uninterpretable.
items.reference: Recognition and Documentation: "Blood should be drawn, whenever possible, before the administration of glucose to allow documentation of a low plasma glucose concentration."

items.question: A patient reports episodes of sweating and confusion. Which finding is required to diagnose a hypoglycemic disorder (Whipple's triad)?
options: Symptoms plus a low plasma glucose plus relief of symptoms when glucose is raised
items.explanation: Whipple's triad requires all three: symptoms consistent with hypoglycemia, a documented low plasma glucose (precise method), and resolution of symptoms when glucose is raised. Symptoms or a low glucose alone is insufficient, and glucometers are not adequate for definitive diagnosis.
items.reference: Intro: "Hypoglycemia may be documented by Whipple's triad: (1) symptoms consistent with hypoglycemia, (2) a low plasma glucose concentration... and (3) relief of symptoms after the plasma glucose level is raised."

items.question: What is the FIRST physiologic defense against a falling plasma glucose?
items.explanation: The first defense is a decrease in insulin secretion, which permits hepatic glycogenolysis and gluconeogenesis. Glucagon and epinephrine are the next (counterregulatory) defenses; cortisol and growth hormone act only late and play no role in acute defense.
items.reference: Counterregulation: "a decrease in insulin secretion is the first defense against hypoglycemia."

items.question: In a patient with long-standing type 1 diabetes whose glucagon response is lost, which hormone becomes the critical defense against acute hypoglycemia?
items.explanation: Epinephrine becomes critical when glucagon is deficient. Cortisol and growth hormone act only after ~4 hours and have no role in acute hypoglycemia defense. When the epinephrine response is also attenuated, defective glucose counterregulation results.
items.reference: Counterregulation: "Epinephrine becomes critical when glucagon is deficient... cortisol and growth hormone play no role in defense against acute hypoglycemia."

items.question: Which symptom is a NEUROGLYCOPENIC (rather than autonomic) manifestation of hypoglycemia?
items.explanation: Confusion, behavioral change, seizure, and loss of consciousness are neuroglycopenic (direct CNS glucose deprivation). Palpitations and tremor are adrenergic, and sweating/hunger are cholinergic — both are neurogenic (autonomic) symptoms.
items.reference: Clinical Manifestations: "Neuroglycopenic manifestations... include behavioral changes, confusion, fatigue, seizure, loss of consciousness."

items.question: What is the recommended approach to restore hypoglycemia awareness in a diabetic with hypoglycemia unawareness?
items.explanation: Because recent antecedent hypoglycemia drives hypoglycemia-associated autonomic failure, 2–3 weeks of scrupulously avoiding hypoglycemia can reverse hypoglycemia unawareness (and partly restore the epinephrine response). Tighter control or more insulin would worsen it; beta-blockers blunt warning symptoms.
items.reference: HAAF: "Hypoglycemia unawareness... can be reversible by as little as 2-3 weeks of scrupulous avoidance of hypoglycemia."

items.question: The failure of glucagon to rise during hypoglycemia in long-standing type 1 diabetes is best explained by which mechanism?
options: A functional signaling defect (alpha cells are present and respond to other stresses like exercise)
items.explanation: Alpha cells are present in normal number/size and glucagon still responds to other stresses (e.g., exercise), so the defect is a functional signaling problem specific to the hypoglycemia response — distinct from structural, irreversible autonomic neuropathy.
items.reference: Defective Counterregulation: "the defect that restricts glucagon release during hypoglycemia in T1DM... appears to be a signaling defect, as glucagon responses to other physiologic stress in T1DM (e.g., exercise) are preserved."

items.question: Which diabetes medication can cause hypoglycemia when used as monotherapy?
items.explanation: Only insulin and insulin secretagogues (sulfonylureas, glinides) cause hypoglycemia on their own. Metformin, SGLT-2 inhibitors, DPP-4 inhibitors, TZDs, α-glucosidase inhibitors, and GLP-1RAs do not cause hypoglycemia alone (though they add risk when combined with insulin/secretagogues).
items.reference: Hypoglycemia in Diabetes: "Insulin, sulfonylureas, or glinides can cause hypoglycemia... Metformin, thiazolidinediones, α-glucosidase inhibitors, GLP-1 receptor agonists, [SGLT-2] inhibitors, and DPP-IV inhibitors do not cause hypoglycemia."

items.question: Why does alcohol cause hypoglycemia, typically after a multi-day binge with poor food intake?
options: It blocks gluconeogenesis (but not glycogenolysis), so hypoglycemia occurs once glycogen stores are depleted
items.explanation: Ethanol blocks gluconeogenesis but not glycogenolysis. After a binge with little food, glycogen is depleted, and with gluconeogenesis blocked, glucose can no longer be maintained. (Glucagon is therefore ineffective in this setting — glycogen is gone.)
items.reference: Drugs: "Ethanol blocks gluconeogenesis but not glycogenolysis. Thus, alcohol-induced hypoglycemia typically occurs after a several-day ethanol binge during which the person eats little food, with consequent glycogen depletion."

items.question: During a documented hypoglycemic episode (glucose 45 mg/dL), which set of results indicates endogenous hyperinsulinism?
options: Low insulin, low C-peptide, high β-hydroxybutyrate
Inappropriately non-suppressed insulin (≥3 µU/mL), C-peptide (≥0.6 ng/mL), and proinsulin, with low β-hydroxybutyrate
Normal insulin with high IGF-II:IGF-I ratio
items.explanation: Endogenous hyperinsulinism is defined by failure of insulin to suppress during hypoglycemia: insulin ≥3 µU/mL, C-peptide ≥0.6 ng/mL, and proinsulin ≥5 pmol/L with glucose <55 mg/dL, plus a low β-hydroxybutyrate and a glucose rise after IV glucagon (insulin action). A high IGF-II:IGF-I ratio with suppressed insulin indicates a non-islet tumor instead.
items.reference: Endogenous Hyperinsulinism: "a plasma insulin concentration ≥3 μU/mL... a plasma C-peptide concentration ≥0.6 ng/mL... and a plasma proinsulin concentration ≥5.0 pmol/L when the plasma glucose concentration is <55 mg/dL."

items.question: A patient has hyperinsulinemic hypoglycemia with HIGH insulin but LOW C-peptide. What is the most likely cause?
items.explanation: Exogenous (injected) insulin contains no C-peptide and suppresses endogenous secretion, so insulin is high but C-peptide is LOW. An insulinoma or sulfonylurea raises BOTH insulin and C-peptide (distinguished by screening for the secretagogue). A non-islet tumor suppresses insulin.
items.reference: Factitious: "exogenous insulin causes hypoglycemia with low C-peptide levels, reflecting suppression of insulin secretion."

items.question: How is sulfonylurea-induced hypoglycemia distinguished from an insulinoma, given both raise insulin and C-peptide?
options: By screening the blood/urine for the circulating secretagogue
items.explanation: Sulfonylureas/glinides produce a clinical and biochemical picture identical to insulinoma (high insulin and C-peptide), but are distinguished by detecting the circulating secretagogue on a sulfonylurea/glinide screen.
items.reference: Endogenous Hyperinsulinism: "The presence of an insulin secretagogue, such as a sulfonylurea or a glinide, results in a clinical and biochemical pattern similar to that of an insulinoma but can be distinguished by the presence of the circulating secretagogue."

items.question: A patient with a large hepatoma has fasting hypoglycemia. Insulin and C-peptide are SUPPRESSED. What is the most likely mechanism?
options: Tumor overproduction of incompletely processed IGF-II ('big IGF-II')
items.explanation: Non-islet-cell tumor hypoglycemia (large mesenchymal/epithelial tumors, hepatomas, adrenocortical carcinomas, carcinoids) is usually due to 'big IGF-II', which acts like insulin while endogenous insulin is appropriately suppressed; the IGF-II:IGF-I ratio is high. Debulking or glucocorticoid/GH can ameliorate it.
items.reference: Non-β-Cell Tumors: "hypoglycemia is due to overproduction of an incompletely processed form of insulin-like growth factor II ('big IGF-II')... insulin secretion is suppressed appropriately during hypoglycemia."

items.question: A patient with alcohol-induced hypoglycemia has no IV access. Why is intramuscular glucagon likely to be ineffective?
options: Glucagon works by stimulating glycogenolysis, and glycogen stores are depleted in alcohol-induced hypoglycemia
items.explanation: Glucagon acts by stimulating glycogenolysis, so it is ineffective when glycogen is depleted — as in alcohol-induced hypoglycemia (after a binge with poor intake). These patients need glucose (oral or IV).
items.reference: Urgent Treatment: "Because it acts by stimulating glycogenolysis, glucagon is ineffective in glycogen-depleted individuals (e.g., those with alcohol-induced hypoglycemia)."

items.question: Which agent can be used to suppress insulin secretion in sulfonylurea-induced hypoglycemia?
items.explanation: Octreotide (a somatostatin analogue) suppresses insulin secretion and is useful in sulfonylurea-induced hypoglycemia, which can otherwise persist for hours to days. Glucose is also given; glucagon is less ideal because it stimulates insulin secretion.
items.reference: Urgent Treatment: "The somatostatin analogue octreotide can be used to suppress insulin secretion in sulfonylurea-induced hypoglycemia."

items.question: Which statement about insulinomas is correct?
options: More than 90% are benign and surgical resection of a solitary tumor is usually curative
They are usually large (>5 cm) and cause mass effects
items.explanation: >90% of insulinomas are benign, >99% are within the pancreas, and most are small (<2 cm), presenting because of hypoglycemia rather than mass effect. Resection of a solitary tumor is generally curative; diazoxide or octreotide is used if unresectable. They can be part of MEN 1 (younger patients).
items.reference: Insulinoma: "Because >90% of insulinomas are benign, they are a treatable cause of potentially fatal hypoglycemia... Surgical resection of a solitary insulinoma is generally curative."

items.question: Which imaging test has the highest sensitivity (~90%) for localizing an insulinoma?
items.explanation: Endoscopic ultrasound has a sensitivity of ~90% for insulinomas. CT/MRI detect ~70–80%; somatostatin receptor scintigraphy detects only about half; intraoperative ultrasound almost always localizes tumors not palpable by the surgeon.
items.reference: Insulinoma: "endoscopic ultrasound has a sensitivity of ~90%."

items.question: After drugs, what is the next most common category of cause of hypoglycemia in hospitalized patients?
options: Critical illness (hepatic, renal, or cardiac failure; sepsis; inanition)
items.explanation: Among hospitalized patients, serious illnesses — hepatic, renal, or cardiac failure, sepsis, and inanition — are second only to drugs as causes of hypoglycemia.
items.reference: Critical Illness: "serious illnesses such as renal, hepatic, or cardiac failure; sepsis; and inanition are second only to drugs as causes of hypoglycemia."

items.question: Why does renal failure predispose to hypoglycemia?
options: It reduces insulin clearance (raising effective insulin) and reduces mobilization of gluconeogenic precursors
items.explanation: Renal failure reduces clearance of insulin (so insulin is inappropriately high relative to glucose) and reduces mobilization of gluconeogenic precursors; the kidney is also itself a site of gluconeogenesis. This is also a risk factor for hypoglycemia in insulin-treated diabetics.
items.reference: Critical Illness: "hypoglycemia in patients with renal failure is also caused by the reduced clearance of insulin... and the reduced mobilization of gluconeogenic precursors in renal failure."

items.question: Which statement about hormone-deficiency hypoglycemia in adults is correct?
options: Isolated epinephrine deficiency reliably causes fasting hypoglycemia
Cortisol and growth hormone are not critical for preventing acute hypoglycemia, but their deficiency can cause hypoglycemia with prolonged fasting (e.g., Addison's, hypopituitarism)
Growth hormone deficiency never causes hypoglycemia at any age
items.explanation: Cortisol and GH are not critical for acute defense, but deficiency (Addison's, hypopituitarism) can cause hypoglycemia with prolonged fasting/stress (glycogen depletion, impaired gluconeogenesis). Isolated epinephrine deficiency alone does not cause hypoglycemia; GH deficiency can cause hypoglycemia in young children.
items.reference: Hormone Deficiencies: "Neither cortisol nor growth hormone is critical to the prevention of hypoglycemia, at least in adults. Nonetheless, hypoglycemia can occur with prolonged fasting in patients with primary adrenocortical failure (Addison's disease) or hypopituitarism."

items.question: A patient develops POSTPRANDIAL hyperinsulinemic hypoglycemia a year after Roux-en-Y gastric bypass. What is the typical underlying finding?
options: Diffuse islet involvement with endogenous hyperinsulinism (e.g., exaggerated GLP-1 responses)
items.explanation: Post-gastric-bypass hypoglycemia (most often after Roux-en-Y) features diffuse islet involvement and endogenous hyperinsulinism with postprandial hypoglycemia; exaggerated GLP-1 responses to meals have been implicated. If medical therapy (acarbose, diazoxide, octreotide) fails, partial pancreatectomy may be required.
items.reference: Endogenous Hyperinsulinism: "Post-gastric bypass postprandial hypoglycemia, which most often follows Roux-en-Y gastric bypass, is also characterized by diffuse islet involvement and endogenous hyperinsulinism."

items.question: A non-diabetic, asymptomatic patient has a surprisingly low laboratory glucose and a markedly elevated white cell count. What should be suspected?
options: Pseudohypoglycemia (ex-vivo glucose consumption by blood cells)
items.explanation: A low measured glucose without symptoms, especially with leukocytosis (or erythrocytosis/thrombocytosis) or delayed serum separation, suggests pseudohypoglycemia — the blood's formed elements keep metabolizing glucose in the tube. Whipple's triad is not met.
items.reference: Pseudohypoglycemia: "low measured glucose concentrations can be a virtual... result of continued glucose metabolism by the formed elements of the blood ex vivo, particularly in the presence of leukocytosis, erythrocytosis, or thrombocytosis."

items.question: A type 1 diabetic with documented hypoglycemia unawareness is at approximately what increased risk of severe iatrogenic hypoglycemia during intensive therapy?
options: Risk is unchanged but symptoms differ
items.explanation: Hypoglycemia unawareness confers about a sixfold increased risk of severe iatrogenic hypoglycemia; defective glucose counterregulation (attenuated epinephrine with absent insulin/glucagon responses) confers a ≥25-fold increased risk.
items.reference: Hypoglycemia Unawareness: "Affected patients are at a sixfold increased risk of severe iatrogenic hypoglycemia during intensive glycemic therapy."

items.question: A conscious, cooperative patient develops symptomatic hypoglycemia. What is the appropriate initial treatment?
options: 15–20 g of oral glucose (tablets, glucose-containing fluid, or food)
items.explanation: If the patient is able and willing, oral glucose ~15–20 g is appropriate. Parenteral therapy (IV glucose 25 g, or IM/SC glucagon if no IV access) is reserved for those unable/unwilling to take oral carbohydrate due to neuroglycopenia. All measures are transient — the patient should eat to replete glycogen.
items.reference: Urgent Treatment: "If the patient is able and willing, oral treatment with glucose tablets or glucose-containing fluids... is appropriate. A reasonable initial dose is 15–20 g of glucose."

items.question: When hypoglycemia is suspected and the cause is unclear, what should be done regarding blood sampling?
options: Give glucose first, then draw labs
Draw blood before giving glucose, when possible, to document the low glucose and measure insulin/C-peptide/proinsulin/β-hydroxybutyrate and a secretagogue screen
Wait until the next morning to draw labs
items.explanation: Whenever possible, blood is drawn before giving glucose so the low glucose is documented and the diagnostic panel (insulin, C-peptide, proinsulin, β-hydroxybutyrate, plus an oral hypoglycemic agent screen) can be measured while the patient is hypoglycemic — values become uninterpretable after treatment.
items.reference: Recognition and Documentation: "Blood should be drawn, whenever possible, before the administration of glucose... additional measurements—made while the glucose level is low and before treatment—should include plasma insulin, C-peptide, proinsulin, and β-hydroxybutyrate levels."

items.question: Which statement about hypoglycemia in type 2 versus type 1 diabetes is correct?
options: Hypoglycemia never occurs in type 2 diabetes
Although incidence is lower in T2DM, the total burden of hypoglycemia is now greater in T2DM because patients far outnumber those with T1DM
Severe hypoglycemia only occurs at HbA1c <6%
Hypoglycemia in T2DM carries no cardiovascular risk
items.explanation: Incidence is lower in T2DM, but prevalence in insulin-requiring T2DM approaches 70%, and because T2DM patients outnumber T1DM by 10–20 fold, the total hypoglycemia burden is now greater in T2DM. Severe hypoglycemia can occur at HbA1c 8–10% and carries increased cardiovascular risk for up to a year.
items.reference: Hypoglycemia in Diabetes: "as patients with T2DM outnumber those with T1DM by 10- to 20-fold, the prevalence of hypoglycemia is now greater in T2DM."

items.question: Why is the brain particularly vulnerable to hypoglycemia?
options: It cannot synthesize glucose or store more than a few minutes' worth, so it needs a continuous arterial glucose supply
items.explanation: The brain is an obligate glucose user that cannot synthesize glucose and stores only minutes' worth as glycogen, so it depends on continuous arterial glucose delivery; when delivery falls, neuroglycopenic symptoms result. (Brain glucose uptake is insulin-independent.)
items.reference: Glucose Balance: "The brain cannot synthesize glucose or store more than a few minutes' supply as glycogen and therefore requires a continuous supply of glucose from the arterial circulation."

items.question: By what mechanism does sepsis cause hypoglycemia?
options: Excess insulin secretion from the pancreas
Cytokine-induced increased glucose utilization plus inhibition of gluconeogenesis, outpacing glucose production
items.explanation: In sepsis, cytokines increase glucose utilization in macrophage-rich tissues (liver, spleen, lung) and inhibit gluconeogenesis; combined with glycogen depletion and hepatic/renal hypoperfusion, glucose production fails to keep pace, causing hypoglycemia.
items.reference: Critical Illness: "Increased glucose utilization is induced by cytokine production in macrophage-rich tissues such as the liver, spleen, and lung. Hypoglycemia develops if glucose production fails to keep pace."

items.question: A patient with chronically poorly controlled diabetes experiences adrenergic symptoms when glucose falls toward the normal range (~90 mg/dL). What explains this?
options: The glycemic threshold for symptoms has shifted upward in poorly controlled diabetes
items.explanation: Glycemic thresholds for counterregulation/symptoms are dynamic: in poorly controlled diabetes they shift upward, so the patient feels hypoglycemic symptoms as glucose merely declines toward normal. (With recurrent hypoglycemia, thresholds shift downward instead.)
items.reference: Counterregulation: "They shift to higher-than-normal glucose levels in people with poorly controlled diabetes, who can experience symptoms of hypoglycemia when their glucose levels decline toward the normal range."

items.question: Which commonly used drug class can both contribute to hypoglycemia and MASK its adrenergic warning symptoms?
items.explanation: Beta-blockers are among the drugs associated with hypoglycemia and additionally blunt adrenergic warning symptoms (palpitations, tremor), which can mask developing hypoglycemia. Other associated drugs include ACE-I/ARB, quinolones, quinine, indomethacin, and sulfonamides.
items.reference: Drugs: "Many other drugs have been associated with hypoglycemia. These include... β-adrenergic receptor antagonists, quinolone antibiotics, indomethacin, quinine, and sulfonamides."

items.question: An insulinoma cannot be completely resected. Which medical therapy is appropriate to control hypoglycemia?
items.explanation: When complete resection is not possible, diazoxide (which inhibits insulin secretion) or the somatostatin analogue octreotide controls hypoglycemia; everolimus (mTOR inhibitor) is an additional option. Insulin and sulfonylureas would worsen hypoglycemia.
items.reference: Insulinoma: "Diazoxide, which inhibits insulin secretion, or the somatostatin analogue octreotide can be used to treat hypoglycemia in patients with unresectable tumors."

items.statement: A normal plasma glucose measured during a symptomatic episode excludes hypoglycemia as the cause of those symptoms.
items.explanation: True. A normal glucose during symptoms excludes hypoglycemia as the cause (Whipple's triad is not met). The ideal time to measure glucose is during a symptomatic episode.
items.reference: Recognition: "A normal glucose level excludes hypoglycemia as the cause of the symptoms."

items.statement: Cortisol and growth hormone are the primary defenses against acute hypoglycemia.
items.explanation: False. The primary defenses are decreased insulin, then glucagon and epinephrine. Cortisol and growth hormone act only after ~4 hours and play no role in acute hypoglycemia defense.
items.reference: Counterregulation: "cortisol and growth hormone play no role in defense against acute hypoglycemia."

items.statement: Metformin commonly causes hypoglycemia when used as monotherapy.
items.explanation: False. Metformin does not cause hypoglycemia alone; only insulin and secretagogues (sulfonylureas, glinides) do. Metformin can add risk when combined with insulin or a secretagogue.
items.reference: Hypoglycemia in Diabetes: "Metformin, thiazolidinediones, α-glucosidase inhibitors, GLP-1 receptor agonists, [SGLT-2] inhibitors, and DPP-IV inhibitors do not cause hypoglycemia."

items.statement: Glucagon is an effective treatment for alcohol-induced hypoglycemia.
items.explanation: False. Glucagon works by stimulating glycogenolysis, but glycogen stores are depleted in alcohol-induced hypoglycemia, so glucagon is ineffective; glucose (oral or IV) is required.
items.reference: Urgent Treatment: "glucagon is ineffective in glycogen-depleted individuals (e.g., those with alcohol-induced hypoglycemia)."

items.statement: Hypoglycemia from surreptitious exogenous insulin shows a LOW C-peptide level.
items.explanation: True. Injected insulin contains no C-peptide and suppresses endogenous secretion, so C-peptide is low. By contrast, insulinoma or a secretagogue raises both insulin and C-peptide.
items.reference: Factitious: "exogenous insulin causes hypoglycemia with low C-peptide levels, reflecting suppression of insulin secretion."

items.statement: Non-islet cell tumor hypoglycemia is typically associated with suppressed insulin secretion and elevated 'big IGF-II'.
items.explanation: True. In non-islet (mesenchymal/epithelial) tumor hypoglycemia, insulin is appropriately suppressed and hypoglycemia results from incompletely processed IGF-II ('big IGF-II') acting on tissues; the IGF-II:IGF-I ratio is high.
items.reference: Non-β-Cell Tumors: "insulin secretion is suppressed appropriately during hypoglycemia... due to overproduction of an incompletely processed form of insulin-like growth factor II ('big IGF-II')."

items.statement: Recent antecedent hypoglycemia blunts the responses to subsequent hypoglycemia, creating a vicious cycle.
items.explanation: True. Recent hypoglycemia (or prior exercise/sleep) reduces the epinephrine and sympathoadrenal responses to the next episode, causing defective counterregulation and hypoglycemia unawareness — a self-perpetuating cycle (HAAF).
items.reference: HAAF: "recent antecedent iatrogenic hypoglycemia... causes both defective glucose counterregulation... and hypoglycemia unawareness... create a vicious cycle of recurrent iatrogenic hypoglycemia."

items.statement: The majority of insulinomas are malignant.
items.explanation: False. More than 90% of insulinomas are benign, and surgical resection of a solitary tumor is generally curative.
items.reference: Insulinoma: "Because >90% of insulinomas are benign, they are a treatable cause of potentially fatal hypoglycemia."

items.statement: Hand-held glucometers and continuous glucose monitors are adequate for the definitive diagnosis of hypoglycemia.
items.explanation: False. Glucometers and CGMs are not quantitative instruments at low glucose and should not be used for definitive diagnosis; a properly handled plasma glucose measured by a precise method is required.
items.reference: Pseudohypoglycemia: "hand-held and continuous glucose monitors... are not quantitative instruments, particularly at low glucose levels, and should not be used for the definitive diagnosis of hypoglycemia."

items.statement: Isolated epinephrine deficiency (with adequate glucocorticoid replacement) reliably causes hypoglycemia.
items.explanation: False. Hypoglycemia is not a feature of the epinephrine-deficient state (e.g., after bilateral adrenalectomy) when glucocorticoid replacement is adequate. It is the COMBINED deficiency of glucagon and epinephrine that drives iatrogenic hypoglycemia in insulin-deficient diabetes.
items.reference: Hormone Deficiencies: "Hypoglycemia is not a feature of the epinephrine-deficient state that results from bilateral adrenalectomy when glucocorticoid replacement is adequate."

items.statement: Severe hypoglycemia is prothrombotic and is associated with increased cardiovascular risk for up to a year afterward.
items.explanation: True. Hypoglycemia activates proinflammatory, procoagulant, and proatherothrombotic responses and impairs nitric-oxide-mediated vasodilation; severe hypoglycemia in T2DM carries increased cardiovascular/cerebrovascular morbidity and mortality for up to a year.
items.reference: Pathophysiology: "Hypoglycemia activates proinflammatory, procoagulant, and proatherothrombotic responses... Severe hypoglycemia in T2DM carries an increased risk of severe cardiovascular and cerebrovascular morbidity and mortality for up to 1 year."

items.statement: In a seemingly well (non-ill, non-medicated) patient with documented hypoglycemia, endogenous hyperinsulinism and factitious causes should be considered.
items.explanation: True. In a seemingly well individual, after excluding drugs/critical illness/hormone deficiency/non-islet tumor, the focus shifts to endogenous hyperinsulinism (insulinoma, nesidioblastosis, post-bypass, autoimmune) and accidental/surreptitious/malicious hypoglycemia.
items.reference: Diagnostic approach: "in a seemingly well individual, the focus should shift to possible endogenous hyperinsulinism or accidental, surreptitious, or even malicious hypoglycemia."

items.statement: Brain glucose uptake is increased by insulin during hypoglycemia.
items.explanation: False. Brain glucose uptake is insulin-independent — it is not altered by insulin, glucagon, epinephrine, cortisol, or growth hormone. The brain simply depends on a continuous arterial glucose supply.
items.reference: Glucose Balance (note): "Rd by the brain is not altered by insulin, glucagon, epinephrine, cortisol, or growth hormone."

items.statement: Treatments such as IV glucose and glucagon raise glucose only transiently, so the patient should eat to replenish glycogen.
items.explanation: True. These measures raise plasma glucose only transiently; patients should be urged to eat as soon as practical to replete glycogen stores and prevent recurrence.
items.reference: Urgent Treatment: "These treatments raise plasma glucose concentrations only transiently, and patients should therefore be urged to eat as soon as is practical to replete glycogen stores."

items.assertion: A decrease in insulin secretion is the first defense against a falling plasma glucose.
items.reason: Lower insulin levels permit hepatic glycogenolysis and gluconeogenesis and reduce peripheral glucose uptake.
items.explanation: Both true and the reason explains the assertion. Falling insulin disinhibits hepatic glucose production and limits peripheral uptake, making it the first line of defense before glucagon and epinephrine are recruited.
items.reference: Counterregulation: "As plasma glucose levels decline... pancreatic β-cell insulin secretion decreases, thereby increasing hepatic glycogenolysis and hepatic (and renal) gluconeogenesis."

items.assertion: A type 1 diabetic with hypoglycemia unawareness should be advised to avoid hypoglycemia for 2–3 weeks.
items.reason: Recent antecedent hypoglycemia blunts the sympathoadrenal warning response, and avoiding lows can reverse this.
items.explanation: Both true and the reason explains the assertion. Because antecedent hypoglycemia causes the unawareness, scrupulous avoidance for 2–3 weeks resets glycemic thresholds and restores warning symptoms.
items.reference: HAAF: "Hypoglycemia unawareness... can be reversible by as little as 2-3 weeks of scrupulous avoidance of hypoglycemia."

items.assertion: Alcohol-induced hypoglycemia tends to occur after a multi-day binge with little food intake.
items.reason: Ethanol blocks gluconeogenesis but not glycogenolysis, so hypoglycemia appears once glycogen stores are depleted.
items.explanation: Both true and the reason explains the assertion. With glycogenolysis intact, glucose is maintained until glycogen runs out; once depleted, the ethanol-blocked gluconeogenesis can no longer sustain glucose.
items.reference: Drugs: "Ethanol blocks gluconeogenesis but not glycogenolysis. Thus, alcohol-induced hypoglycemia typically occurs after a several-day ethanol binge during which the person eats little food, with consequent glycogen depletion."

items.assertion: A low C-peptide in a patient with hyperinsulinemic hypoglycemia points to exogenous insulin administration.
items.reason: Manufactured insulin contains no C-peptide and suppresses the patient's own insulin secretion.
items.explanation: Both true and the reason explains the assertion. Because injected insulin lacks C-peptide and suppresses endogenous secretion, a high insulin with low C-peptide indicates exogenous insulin rather than an insulinoma/secretagogue.
items.reference: Factitious: "exogenous insulin causes hypoglycemia with low C-peptide levels, reflecting suppression of insulin secretion."

items.assertion: The impaired glucagon response to hypoglycemia in long-standing type 1 diabetes is potentially reversible.
items.reason: It reflects a functional signaling defect rather than loss of pancreatic alpha cells.
items.explanation: Both true and the reason explains the assertion. Alpha cells are intact and respond to other stresses, so the hypoglycemia-specific glucagon failure is functional (HAAF), distinguishing it from irreversible structural autonomic neuropathy.
items.reference: Defective Counterregulation: "the defect that restricts glucagon release during hypoglycemia in T1DM... appears to be a signaling defect... This functional—and potentially reversible—disorder is distinct from classic diabetic autonomic neuropathy."

items.assertion: In non-islet cell tumor hypoglycemia, plasma insulin and C-peptide are suppressed.
items.reason: Hypoglycemia is mediated by tumor-derived 'big IGF-II' rather than by insulin.
items.explanation: Both true and the reason explains the assertion. Because the hypoglycemia is driven by incompletely processed IGF-II acting on tissues, endogenous insulin secretion is appropriately suppressed (low insulin and C-peptide).
items.reference: Non-β-Cell Tumors: "insulin secretion is suppressed appropriately during hypoglycemia... due to overproduction of an incompletely processed form of insulin-like growth factor II ('big IGF-II')."

items.assertion: Octreotide is useful in sulfonylurea-induced hypoglycemia.
items.reason: Octreotide is a somatostatin analogue that suppresses insulin secretion.
items.explanation: Both true and the reason explains the assertion. By suppressing the sulfonylurea-driven insulin secretion, octreotide helps prevent the prolonged/recurrent hypoglycemia these drugs can cause.
items.reference: Urgent Treatment: "The somatostatin analogue octreotide can be used to suppress insulin secretion in sulfonylurea-induced hypoglycemia."

items.assertion: Renal failure increases the risk of hypoglycemia in insulin-treated diabetics.
items.reason: Reduced renal clearance of insulin raises effective insulin levels relative to the prevailing glucose.
items.explanation: Both true and the reason explains the assertion. Impaired insulin clearance (plus reduced mobilization of gluconeogenic precursors) inappropriately increases insulin action, predisposing to hypoglycemia.
items.reference: Critical Illness: "hypoglycemia in patients with renal failure is also caused by the reduced clearance of insulin (thereby inappropriately increasing insulin relative to the prevailing glucose levels)."

items.assertion: A low laboratory glucose in an asymptomatic patient with marked leukocytosis may be artifactual.
items.reason: Blood cells continue to metabolize glucose ex vivo after the sample is drawn.
items.explanation: Both true and the reason explains the assertion. Ongoing ex-vivo glycolysis by the formed elements (especially with leukocytosis/erythrocytosis/thrombocytosis or delayed processing) lowers the measured glucose, producing pseudohypoglycemia.
items.reference: Pseudohypoglycemia: "the result of continued glucose metabolism by the formed elements of the blood ex vivo, particularly in the presence of leukocytosis, erythrocytosis, or thrombocytosis."

items.assertion: Neuroglycopenic symptoms develop when plasma glucose falls below the physiologic range.
items.reason: The brain cannot store or synthesize meaningful glucose and depends on continuous arterial delivery.
items.explanation: Both true and the reason explains the assertion. Because the brain has minimal glucose reserves, falling arterial glucose reduces blood-to-brain transport below what brain metabolism needs, producing neuroglycopenia.
items.reference: Glucose Balance: "As the arterial plasma glucose concentration falls below the physiologic range, blood-to-brain glucose transport becomes insufficient to support brain energy metabolism and function."

items.assertion: Glycemic improvement in high-cardiovascular-risk type 2 diabetics should be done incrementally.
items.reason: Severe hypoglycemia activates procoagulant and proatherothrombotic pathways and raises cardiovascular risk.
items.explanation: Both true and the reason explains the assertion. Because hypoglycemia is prothrombotic and associated with cardiovascular events, aggressive HbA1c lowering (risking hypoglycemia) can be harmful in high-risk patients, so it is done carefully and incrementally.
items.reference: Hypoglycemia in Diabetes: "the risk of severe hypoglycemia and a subsequent cardiovascular adverse event is... relatively increased when trying to improve glucose control in some T2DM individuals... improvements in glycemic control in these individuals should be performed incrementally."

items.assertion: Cortisol deficiency can cause fasting hypoglycemia.
items.reason: Cortisol deficiency impairs gluconeogenesis and is associated with low gluconeogenic precursor levels and glycogen depletion.
items.explanation: Both true and the reason explains the assertion. Although cortisol is not an acute defense, its deficiency impairs gluconeogenesis and (with anorexia-related glycogen depletion) leads to substrate-limited gluconeogenesis, causing hypoglycemia during fasting.
items.reference: Hormone Deficiencies: "Cortisol deficiency is associated with impaired gluconeogenesis and low levels of gluconeogenic precursors; these associations suggest that substrate-limited gluconeogenesis, in the setting of glycogen depletion, is the cause of hypoglycemia."
